expanded_description: Understand how the number of shared electron pairs between atoms creates single, double, and triple covalent bonds, and how bond order affects bond length, strength, and molecular properties.

core_concept.explanation: Covalent bonds are classified by the number of electron pairs shared between two atoms. Single bonds share one pair, double bonds share two pairs, and triple bonds share three pairs. Increasing bond order creates stronger, shorter bonds with distinct chemical reactivity.

image.alt: Diagram comparing single, double, and triple bond structures
image.caption: Figure 6.2.1: Bond order comparison—single ($\sigma$), double ($\sigma + \pi$), and triple ($\sigma + 2\pi$) bonds showing increasing electron density between nuclei.

sections.introduction: Single covalent bonds form when two atoms share exactly one pair of electrons. These are the most common bonds in organic molecules and provide the basic framework for molecular structure.

key_concept.definition: A single covalent bond consists of one shared electron pair ($2$ electrons total) between two atoms, represented by a single line ($-$) in structural formulas.
key_concept.context: All single bonds are sigma ($\sigma$) bonds, formed by head-on orbital overlap along the internuclear axis.

characteristics: Allows free rotation (in absence of other constraints)
classifications.behavior: Electron density is concentrated along the line connecting the two nuclei, providing maximum attractive force.

examples.explanation: Two $1s$ orbitals overlap head-on, sharing one electron pair.
Carbon $sp^3$ orbital overlaps with hydrogen $1s$ orbital.

classifications.value: Longest and Weakest Covalent Bond Type
characteristics: Free rotation possible around bond axis
classifications.behavior: Single bonds are more flexible and reactive than multiple bonds, allowing conformational changes in molecules.

principles.statement: Single ($\sigma$) bonds allow free rotation around the bond axis because the cylindrical symmetry of orbital overlap is maintained during rotation.
principles.application: Ethane ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$) can rotate around its $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bond, creating different conformations (staggered, eclipsed) without breaking the bond.
principles.analogy: Like two spheres connected by a rod—you can spin either sphere without affecting the connection.

sections.introduction: Double covalent bonds form when two atoms share two pairs of electrons. These bonds are shorter and stronger than single bonds but restrict molecular rotation.

key_concept.definition: A double covalent bond consists of two shared electron pairs ($4$ electrons total), represented by a double line ($=$) in structural formulas.
key_concept.context: Double bonds consist of one sigma ($\sigma$) bond plus one pi ($\pi$) bond.

characteristics: Electron density above and below bond axis
classifications.behavior: The $\pi$ bond restricts rotation because breaking the side-by-side orbital alignment would require significant energy.

examples.explanation: Carbon-oxygen double bond found in aldehydes, ketones, carboxylic acids.

classifications.behavior: Double bonds create rigid molecular regions, enabling cis-trans (geometric) isomerism.

examples.explanation: Bond length $134~\text{pm}$, bond energy $614~\text{kJ/mol}$—shorter and stronger than $\text{C}-\text{C}$.

image.caption: Comparison of double bond length vs. single bond.

principles.statement: The $\pi$ bond's side-by-side orbital overlap prevents free rotation, locking substituents in fixed positions relative to the double bond.
principles.application: 2-butene exists as two distinct isomers: cis-2-butene (methyl groups on same side) and trans-2-butene (methyl groups on opposite sides).
principles.example: Cis-trans isomerism affects physical properties: cis-2-butene bp $3.7^\circ\text{C}$, trans-2-butene bp $0.9^\circ\text{C}$.

principles.statement: $\pi$ bonds are more reactive than $\sigma$ bonds because $\pi$ electrons are less tightly held and more exposed.
principles.application: Alkenes undergo addition reactions (e.g., hydrogenation, halogenation) where the $\pi$ bond breaks but the $\sigma$ bond remains.
principles.analogy: Like a weak link in a chain—the $\pi$ bond breaks first during chemical reactions, leaving the $\sigma$ framework intact.

sections.introduction: Triple covalent bonds form when two atoms share three pairs of electrons. These are the shortest and strongest covalent bonds, found primarily in nitrogen and carbon compounds.

key_concept.definition: A triple covalent bond consists of three shared electron pairs ($6$ electrons total), represented by a triple line ($\equiv$) in structural formulas.
key_concept.context: Triple bonds consist of one sigma ($\sigma$) bond plus two pi ($\pi$) bonds oriented perpendicular to each other.

characteristics: Cylindrical electron density around bond axis
classifications.behavior: The two perpendicular $\pi$ bonds create a cylindrical electron cloud around the $\sigma$ bond axis, making triple bonds linear.

examples.explanation: Extremely strong triple bond makes $\text{N}_2$ chemically inert at room temperature.

image.caption: $\text{N}\equiv\text{N}$ triple bond with one $\sigma$ and two $\pi$ bonds.

examples.explanation: Linear geometry ($180^\circ$ bond angle) due to $sp$ hybridization.

image.caption: Linear structure of ethyne with $\text{C}\equiv\text{C}$ triple bond.

classifications.value: Shortest and Strongest Covalent Bond Type
classifications.behavior: Triple bonds create rigid, linear molecular segments with very high bond dissociation energies.

examples.explanation: Bond length $120~\text{pm}$, bond energy $839~\text{kJ/mol}$—shortest and strongest carbon-carbon bond.

image.caption: Triple bond is $\sim 22\%$ shorter than single $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bond.

principles.statement: Atoms involved in triple bonds adopt $sp$ hybridization, resulting in $180^\circ$ bond angles and linear molecular geometry.
principles.application: Ethyne ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$) is linear: $\text{H}-\text{C}\equiv\text{C}-\text{H}$ with all four atoms in a straight line.
principles.example: Hydrogen cyanide ($\text{H}-\text{C}\equiv\text{N}$) is also linear due to the $\text{C}\equiv\text{N}$ triple bond.

principles.statement: The $\text{N}\equiv\text{N}$ triple bond ($945~\text{kJ/mol}$) is one of the strongest covalent bonds, explaining nitrogen gas's chemical inertness.
principles.application: Breaking $\text{N}_2$ requires high temperature, pressure, or catalysts (e.g., Haber process for ammonia synthesis).
principles.analogy: Like a triple-stranded rope—much harder to break than a single strand, requiring specialized tools (catalysts) to cut.

sections.section_title: Bond Order, Length, and Energy Relationships
sections.introduction: Bond order (number of shared electron pairs) systematically affects bond length and bond energy, allowing predictions of molecular stability and reactivity.

key_concept.definition: Bond order is the number of chemical bonds between a pair of atoms: single=1, double=2, triple=3. Higher bond order correlates with shorter bond length and higher bond energy.
key_concept.context: These relationships are fundamental to understanding molecular structure, stability, and reaction mechanisms.

components.definition: Quantitative relationships between bond order, length, and energy
components.equation: $\text{Bond Order} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Shared Electron Pairs}}{\text{Number of Bond Locations}}$
components.interpretation: Higher bond order pulls nuclei closer together (shorter bond) and requires more energy to break (stronger bond).

image.alt: Graph showing bond order vs. length and energy
image.caption: Figure 6.2.2: Inverse relationship between bond order and length; direct relationship between bond order and energy.

categories.description: Bond length decreases as bond order increases because more shared electrons create stronger attraction between nuclei.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Additional electron pairs increase electron density between nuclei, pulling them closer against nuclear-nuclear repulsion.

image.caption: Bond length decreases $\sim 22\%$ from single to triple.
Double bond $\sim 16\%$ shorter than single bond.

categories.description: Bond energy increases as bond order increases because more shared electrons create stronger electrostatic attraction.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Breaking multiple bonds requires breaking both $\sigma$ and $\pi$ components; $\pi$ bonds add significant stability despite being individually weaker than $\sigma$ bonds.

image.caption: Triple bond requires $\sim 2.4\times$ energy of single bond to break.
Extreme stability of $\text{N}_2$ due to triple bond.

categories.description: While total bond energy increases with bond order, the energy per electron pair actually decreases.
categories.detailed_mechanism: The first ($\sigma$) bond is strongest; each additional $\pi$ bond contributes less energy due to less effective orbital overlap.

examples.explanation: Single: $347~\text{kJ/mol}$ per pair; Double: $307~\text{kJ/mol}$ per pair; Triple: $280~\text{kJ/mol}$ per pair.

image.caption: Diminishing returns on additional bond order.

principles.statement: Shorter bonds are generally stronger because nuclei are held closer together by greater electron density, requiring more energy to separate them.
principles.application: The $\text{C}\equiv\text{C}$ bond is both the shortest ($120~\text{pm}$) and strongest ($839~\text{kJ/mol}$) carbon-carbon bond.
principles.analogy: Like a spring: a tighter, shorter spring (triple bond) requires more force to stretch than a loose, long spring (single bond).

principles.statement: Each $\pi$ bond adds less energy than the $\sigma$ bond because side-by-side $p$-orbital overlap is less effective than head-on overlap.
principles.application: Double bond energy ($614~\text{kJ/mol}$) is less than $2\times$ single bond energy ($694~\text{kJ/mol}$), showing $\pi$ bond is weaker than $\sigma$.
principles.example: This explains why $\pi$ bonds break first in addition reactions while $\sigma$ bonds remain intact.

sections.introduction: Apply bond order principles to predict bond properties, compare molecular stability, and understand reactivity patterns.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Bond Lengths
worked_examples.problem: Rank the following bonds in order of increasing bond length: $\text{C}-\text{C}$, $\text{C}=\text{C}$, $\text{C}\equiv\text{C}$. Explain your reasoning.

step_by_step.explanation: Count the number of shared electron pairs: single=1, double=2, triple=3.
Higher bond order = more electron density = stronger attraction = shorter bond.

worked_examples.final_answer: Increasing bond length: $\text{C}\equiv\text{C} < \text{C}=\text{C} < \text{C}-\text{C}$. Triple bonds are shortest due to highest electron density pulling nuclei closest together.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Bond Order and Bond Length Relationship

worked_examples.title: Predicting Bond Strength
worked_examples.problem: Which molecule is more stable: $\text{N}_2$ or $\text{O}_2$? Use bond order and bond energy to justify your answer.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw Lewis structures to count shared electron pairs.
Higher bond order correlates with higher bond dissociation energy.
More energy required to break the bond = greater molecular stability.

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{N}_2$ is more stable than $\text{O}_2$ because its triple bond ($945~\text{kJ/mol}$) requires nearly twice the energy to break compared to $\text{O}_2$'s double bond ($498~\text{kJ/mol}$). This explains why nitrogen gas is chemically inert at room temperature.

worked_examples.title: Understanding Reactivity Differences
worked_examples.problem: Why do alkenes ($\text{C}=\text{C}$) undergo addition reactions more readily than alkanes ($\text{C}-\text{C}$), even though double bonds are stronger overall?

step_by_step.explanation: Double bonds contain one $\sigma$ and one $\pi$ bond; single bonds contain only $\sigma$.
$\pi$ bonds are weaker than $\sigma$ bonds due to less effective side-by-side orbital overlap.
$\pi$ electrons are located above and below the bond axis, more exposed to electrophilic attack.
Addition reactions break the weaker $\pi$ bond while preserving the stronger $\sigma$ bond.

worked_examples.final_answer: Alkenes are more reactive because the $\pi$ bond component of the double bond is weaker ($\sim 267~\text{kJ/mol}$) and more exposed than $\sigma$ bonds. Addition reactions break only the $\pi$ bond while preserving the $\sigma$ framework, making the overall process energetically favorable despite the double bond's higher total energy.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Pi Bond Reactivity vs. Sigma Bond Stability

worked_examples.title: Bond Energy Calculation
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the approximate $\pi$ bond energy in a $\text{C}=\text{C}$ double bond given: $\text{C}-\text{C}$ single bond = $347~\text{kJ/mol}$, $\text{C}=\text{C}$ double bond = $614~\text{kJ/mol}$.

step_by_step.explanation: Subtract single bond energy from double bond energy to isolate $\pi$ contribution.
Compare $\pi$ bond energy to $\sigma$ bond energy.

worked_examples.final_answer: The $\pi$ bond energy in $\text{C}=\text{C}$ is approximately $267~\text{kJ/mol}$, which is $\sim 77\%$ of the $\sigma$ bond energy ($347~\text{kJ/mol}$). This explains why $\pi$ bonds are more reactive and break first in chemical reactions.

summary: Covalent bonds are classified by bond order: single (1 pair, $\sigma$ only), double (2 pairs, $\sigma + \pi$), and triple (3 pairs, $\sigma + 2\pi$). Increasing bond order decreases bond length and increases total bond energy, but each additional $\pi$ bond contributes less energy than the $\sigma$ bond. Single bonds allow free rotation; double and triple bonds restrict rotation, enabling geometric isomerism. $\pi$ bonds are weaker and more reactive than $\sigma$ bonds, explaining why alkenes undergo addition reactions while alkanes do not. The $\text{N}\equiv\text{N}$ triple bond ($945~\text{kJ/mol}$) is among the strongest covalent bonds, making nitrogen gas exceptionally stable.
